content: As vibe coding and AI-assisted development become mainstream, the IT department transforms from a cost center into the most strategically important team in any organization. Companies can now build tailor-made internal software without large engineering teams, meaning the people who understand both the business need and the technical tools become disproportionately valuable. The organizations that recognize this shift early and invest in their internal build capacity will develop moats that are invisible to competitors until it is too late.
implication: Leaders should stop treating IT as overhead and start treating it as a product studio. The internal tools your team builds today are tomorrow's operational advantage.
source_ref: 2026 Predictions with Scott Belsky

content: The commoditization of software development means that the bottleneck shifts from writing code to knowing what to build. In the enterprise context, this elevates people with domain expertise and taste — those who understand workflows deeply enough to specify exactly what a custom tool should do — above people who can only execute on someone else's specification. The business analyst who can also direct an AI to build becomes one of the most valuable roles in any organization.
implication: Invest in training domain experts to become AI-directed builders. The combination of workflow knowledge and AI fluency is rarer and more valuable than either skill alone.
source_ref: How To Do More With Less People - Scott Belsky

content: Belsky has argued that small teams augmented by AI can now accomplish what previously required hundreds of people — a dynamic he frames as the rise of the Small Team, Big Business model. In an enterprise context, this same principle applies to internal tooling: a two- or three-person team with access to AI coding tools can deploy workflow automation, custom dashboards, and agentic processes that previously required a dedicated software department. The cost of custom enterprise software collapses toward zero.
implication: Budget assumptions for internal software projects need to be completely rethought. What previously required a six-month, six-figure IT project may now take days and cost almost nothing.
source_ref: How To Do More With Less People - Scott Belsky

content: Agentic tools deployed inside organizations are beginning to replace entire categories of knowledge work rather than merely assist with them. Belsky has described this as a qualitative shift from AI as a copilot to AI as an autonomous operator — agents that don't just surface information but take action, complete workflows, and report back on outcomes. The organizational implication is that headcount planning must now account for work being done by systems rather than people.
implication: Start mapping your knowledge workflows to identify which ones are candidates for full agent replacement versus human-in-the-loop assistance. These are different design problems with different build priorities.
source_ref: Scott Belsky & Steve Jang: From AI Chatbots to Copilots

content: Belsky has articulated a progression from AI chatbots to copilots to fully autonomous agents, and has positioned the copilot-to-agent transition as the most consequential leap for enterprise users. Copilots still require humans to direct every action and review every output; agents are trusted with sequences of decisions and can operate across multiple systems without step-by-step oversight. Organizations that have not built the internal trust frameworks — both technical and cultural — to deploy agents at this level will fall behind those that have.
implication: The infrastructure work needed to safely deploy autonomous agents — data access controls, audit trails, escalation protocols — should be started now, before you think you need it.
source_ref: Scott Belsky & Steve Jang: From AI Chatbots to Copilots

content: Belsky has emphasized that the interface layer, not the underlying model, is where value is captured in AI-powered products — and this principle applies with particular force to internal enterprise tools. An AI model accessed through a generic chat interface will be used generically; the same model embedded in a purpose-built workflow tool that meets employees where they already work will drive transformative adoption. The organization that invests in building the right interface around AI captures far more value than one that simply provides API access.
implication: Don't just give employees access to AI — build the interface that makes AI feel like a natural extension of the specific work they already do. Interface design is not a cosmetic decision; it is a strategic one.
source_ref: Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)

content: Belsky has observed that as AI makes code abundant, the security surface area of every organization expands dramatically. When anyone in a company can build and deploy software — through vibe coding or AI-assisted development — the perimeter that IT and security teams must defend grows faster than any centralized team can manage. This creates a new class of infrastructure risk that most organizations have not yet priced into their AI adoption strategies.
implication: Security and governance frameworks need to scale with your AI build capacity, not lag behind it. Every new internal tool is a new attack surface; the policies for governing internally built AI software must be established before the tools proliferate.
source_ref: 2026 Predictions with Scott Belsky

content: Belsky has described the coming wave of AI-native enterprise software as creating disposable or ephemeral software — applications built for a specific moment, workflow, or team that are meant to be replaced rather than maintained. This is a fundamental departure from the traditional enterprise software model of long procurement cycles and multi-year licensing, and it requires a completely different mental model for how organizations think about software investment and depreciation.
implication: Build internal tools with the assumption they will be replaced in months, not years. Design for speed and specificity over permanence and generality — and stop applying legacy procurement logic to AI-native software decisions.
source_ref: 2026 Predictions with Scott Belsky

content: Revenue per employee has emerged in Belsky's framework as the defining metric of the AI-augmented enterprise. He has argued that organizations deploying AI effectively should expect dramatic increases in output per person rather than modest efficiency gains — and that companies still measuring AI success by time saved on individual tasks are measuring the wrong thing entirely. The strategic question is not whether AI is saving hours but whether AI is changing the denominator in the revenue-per-employee equation.
implication: Reframe your AI ROI metrics. If your AI investments are not meaningfully moving revenue per employee, you are probably deploying AI tactically rather than structurally.
source_ref: How To Do More With Less People - Scott Belsky

content: Belsky has consistently argued that seat-based SaaS pricing is structurally broken in an agentic world, because AI agents do not occupy seats and cannot be priced the same way human users are. For enterprise buyers, this creates an immediate and significant leverage opportunity: as AI agents begin doing work previously done by licensed human users, the cost basis of enterprise software should fall, and buyers should be pushing vendors aggressively toward outcome-based pricing models. Companies that fail to renegotiate their vendor relationships in light of this shift are leaving money on the table.
implication: Audit every per-seat SaaS contract your organization holds. Any workflow where AI is now doing work previously done by a licensed human is a negotiation opportunity — and a signal that you should be evaluating outcome-priced alternatives.
source_ref: 2026 Predictions with Scott Belsky

content: Belsky has argued that the most dangerous organizational response to AI abundance is adding process rather than adding judgment. As AI tools proliferate inside enterprises, the instinct is to layer governance, approval workflows, and compliance procedures on top of them. But in his framework, process is a symptom of misalignment, not a solution to it — and organizations that respond to the complexity of AI deployment with more process will slow down precisely when they need to move fast.
implication: Build AI governance around clear principles and decision rights, not around approval chains and checklists. Teams that are genuinely aligned on what they are trying to accomplish need very little process to deploy AI responsibly.
source_ref: The Messy Middle

content: Belsky has noted that the creative tools revolution pioneered in consumer-facing software — real-time collaboration, AI generation, direct manipulation interfaces — is now migrating into enterprise workflow software. Adobe's own trajectory under his product leadership reflected this: the company consistently pushed the frontier of AI-assisted creative tools before many enterprise software vendors had begun serious AI integration. The consumer-to-enterprise migration of AI tooling means that the most advanced internal tools often look more like creative software than like traditional business applications.
implication: Look to consumer-grade AI tools for product inspiration when designing internal enterprise systems. The interaction paradigms being established in creative software will define what enterprise users expect next.
source_ref: Adobe's Scott Belsky: Art & design in the age of AI | Rapid Response

content: In Belsky's view, the first mile principle applies as much to internal enterprise tools as it does to consumer products. Enterprise software has historically gotten away with terrible onboarding because employees had no choice but to use mandated systems. But as employees gain access to AI tools and vibe-coded alternatives, the bar for internal tool adoption rises sharply — workers will route around clunky systems, and shadow IT built with AI will proliferate. Internal product teams now have to compete for employee attention, not just mandate it.
implication: Apply consumer-grade product thinking to internal tools. If your enterprise software cannot hook employees in the first 30 seconds, they will build or find something better — with or without IT's blessing.
source_ref: Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)

content: Belsky has framed taste as the scarce resource in an AI-abundant world, and this applies directly to enterprise AI deployment: the question is no longer whether your organization can build an AI tool but whether you have people with sufficient taste to know which tools are worth building and which workflows are worth automating. Organizations that can consistently make the right build/buy/automate decisions will compound their AI advantage, while those that automate indiscriminately will create technical debt and organizational confusion at AI speed.
implication: Identify the people in your organization with the strongest combination of workflow knowledge and discernment — they are your de facto AI product managers, whether or not they have that title.
source_ref: Interview with Scott Belsky: GenAI - Creativity as the Now Productivity

content: Belsky has observed that AI is transforming what it means to be a knowledge worker by collapsing the gap between thinking and making. In an enterprise context, this means that the employee who used to spend most of their time executing on a plan — writing reports, building presentations, coding analyses — can now spend most of their time on the judgment and direction work that was previously reserved for senior roles. The org chart implications of this shift are profound and most organizations have not begun to reckon with them.
implication: Redesign roles around judgment and direction, not execution. The knowledge worker job description of 2025 should look nothing like it did in 2020 — and if yours still does, you are underusing your people and your AI.
source_ref: Scott Belsky on AI and the Return of a Hyper-Personalized World

content: Belsky has argued that resourcefulness — the capacity to do more with less — is a muscle built through constraint, and that organizations which develop genuine resourcefulness through early AI adoption will outperform those that simply throw AI tools at existing headcount. The risk in enterprise AI adoption is not moving too fast but moving too comfortably: using AI to make existing expensive processes slightly cheaper rather than using AI to fundamentally reimagine what those processes should be.
implication: Use AI as an opportunity to question whether workflows should exist at all, not just to make existing workflows faster. The most valuable AI wins in enterprise come from elimination, not optimization.
source_ref: How To Do More With Less People - Scott Belsky

content: Belsky has described the prompt interface as a transitional — not terminal — form for AI interaction, and has anticipated a shift toward direct manipulation controls that feel like natural extensions of existing workflows. In enterprise software, this suggests that the current generation of AI tools that require employees to learn prompt engineering are not the final form: the winning internal tools will be the ones that embed AI assistance directly into the workflows employees already use, requiring no new interaction paradigm at all.
implication: Prioritize AI features that feel invisible over ones that require employees to learn a new way of working. The most adopted enterprise AI will be the AI that employees barely notice they are using.
source_ref: Adobe's Scott Belsky: Art & design in the age of AI | Rapid Response

content: Belsky has pointed to the convergence of AI capabilities with cloud infrastructure as the enabling condition for a new wave of enterprise software creation. The same forces that allowed Behance to be built by a tiny team — cheap infrastructure, open APIs, accessible design tools — are now available for AI-native internal tool building at a scale that makes custom enterprise software economically viable for any organization, not just large ones with dedicated engineering departments.
implication: The playbook for internal tool building that previously only applied to tech companies now applies to every organization. The question is not whether you can afford to build custom tools — it is whether you can afford not to.
source_ref: Building Adobe & the Future of Design with Scott Belsky

content: Belsky has consistently warned against the organizational tendency to over-explain rather than over-do — what he frames as the Do-Show-Explain hierarchy. This principle has sharp implications for enterprise AI deployment: most internal AI rollouts are dominated by training sessions, documentation, and change management communications rather than by simply doing the work for employees and letting them experience the benefit directly. The organizations that let AI demonstrate its value through action rather than explanation will achieve faster and more durable adoption.
implication: Redesign your internal AI rollout strategy around demonstration rather than education. Build one workflow that AI completes entirely and show employees the output before you explain how it works.
source_ref: Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)

content: Belsky has observed that the most dangerous phase of any new technology deployment inside a large organization is the messy middle — the period after initial excitement when the hard integration work begins and early results are mixed. Enterprise AI adoption is currently in this phase for most organizations: the pilots went well, the board is excited, but the actual embedding of AI into core workflows has produced more friction than expected. The organizations that endure through this phase rather than retreating to safety will emerge with durable advantages.
implication: Treat uneven AI adoption results as a signal to persist and problem-solve, not to pull back. The messy middle of AI transformation is where most competitors will quit — which is precisely why staying in it is strategically valuable.
source_ref: The Messy Middle

content: Belsky has highlighted that as AI agents begin operating with more autonomy inside organizations, the human roles that remain most valuable are those involving oversight, curation, and taste — deciding which agent outputs are good enough to act on, which workflows need human review, and which automated decisions require escalation. This is a fundamentally different kind of managerial work than most organizations have trained their people for, and building this capacity is itself a strategic investment.
implication: Start developing 'agent manager' capabilities in your most trusted operators now. The skill of evaluating, directing, and correcting AI agent behavior is not intuitive — it requires deliberate training and experience.
source_ref: Scott Belsky & Steve Jang: From AI Chatbots to Copilots

content: Belsky has noted that the proliferation of AI tools inside organizations creates a new kind of alignment risk: when every team is building its own AI-assisted workflows and tools, the organizational knowledge that used to live in shared processes and systems becomes fragmented across dozens of locally optimized solutions. The companies that will extract the most value from internal AI building are those that pair the freedom to build locally with the discipline to integrate and share learnings across teams.
implication: Build an internal AI commons — a place where teams document what they have built, what worked, and what failed. Distributed building without knowledge sharing produces expensive duplication rather than compounding advantage.
source_ref: 2026 Predictions with Scott Belsky

content: Belsky has framed Adobe's own transformation as a case study in how a legacy creative software company can reinvent itself around AI-native tools without losing the trust of its core user base. The key was integrating AI into existing workflows in ways that felt like natural extensions of the tools creatives already used — Photoshop, Premiere, Firefly — rather than launching standalone AI products that required behavior change. This inside-out approach to AI integration applies directly to enterprise software strategy: augment the tools employees already trust rather than asking them to adopt new ones.
implication: Map your AI deployment strategy to tools and workflows that already have high internal adoption. The path of least resistance for enterprise AI is through beloved existing tools, not through mandated new ones.
source_ref: Adobe's Scott Belsky: Art & design in the age of AI | Rapid Response
